plan.title: The Right to Disconnect
plan.subtitle: For the one who is so tired she wants to turn everything off, go quiet, and stop responding for a while.
plan.goal: For the one who is so tired she wants to turn everything off, go quiet, and stop responding for a while.

days.title: I Am Not Distant; I Have Been Online for Too Long

verse.text: And he said unto them, Come ye yourselves apart into a desert place, and rest a while: for there were many coming and going, and they had no leisure so much as to eat.

sections.heading: Guided Reflection
paragraphs: Burnout often begins long before a woman has words for it. It begins in the habit of never fully stepping away. You answer work, then family, then messages, then emotional tension, then tomorrow’s concerns. Even silence is crowded. Many women know what it is like to be reachable all day and still feel guilty for wanting one quiet hour in which no one needs anything from them.

That is why Jesus’ invitation in Mark 6 feels so tender. He sees His disciples worn down by constant demand and tells them to come away and rest. Scripture even says they did not have time to eat. God does not ignore that kind of depletion. Psalm 23 gives the same comfort in another form. The Shepherd not only leads; He makes His sheep lie down and restores their souls. **Sometimes the desire to disconnect is not selfishness. It is a sign that your soul has been living without enough pasture.**

Some exhaustion is not solved by trying harder to be kind, available, or efficient. It is eased when you stop treating constant access as holiness. Many women have quietly learned to confuse being dependable with being perpetually open. But those are not the same thing. Love offered from depletion begins to lose warmth. Attention scattered in every direction cannot stay deep for long.

Perhaps the truer spiritual question is not, “Why do I want to step back?” but, “How long have I been living without rest?” There is wisdom in learning when to set the phone down, when to answer later, and when to let silence remain silence. **A faithful life is not one in which you are reachable at every moment. It is one in which your soul remains shepherded by God.**

sections.heading: Prayer
sections.body: Father, I come to You and admit that I am tired. I have often treated constant availability as faithfulness, and I have forgotten that I also need rest. Thank You for being my Shepherd, not only when I am strong, but also when I am worn down. Forgive me for confusing busyness with maturity. Lead me beside still waters and teach me not to fear the pause. Let my heart grow quiet before You, and let wiser boundaries begin to form in my life. In Jesus’ name, amen.

verses.text: And he said unto them, Come ye yourselves apart into a desert place, and rest a while: for there were many coming and going, and they had no leisure so much as to eat.
The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want. He maketh me to lie down in green pastures: he leadeth me beside the still waters. He restoreth my soul.

days.title: God Does Not Pull Away When I Need to Pause

verse.text: And as he lay and slept under a juniper tree, behold, then an angel touched him, and said unto him, Arise and eat… And he arose, and did eat and drink, and went in the strength of that meat forty days and forty nights.

sections.heading: Guided Reflection
paragraphs: Many people are not most afraid of tiredness itself. They are afraid of what stopping might uncover. If you have spent a long time holding life together, pause can feel dangerous. Unfelt sorrow may rise. Anger may surface. You may have to admit that you are not coping as well as everyone assumes. For a woman used to being dependable, even that admission can feel like loss.

Elijah’s story is beautiful because God does not answer collapse with rebuke. He answers it with care. Elijah sleeps. He eats. He is tended before he is redirected. Psalm 103 says that God remembers we are dust. That is not a dismissive statement. It is a compassionate one. **God does not expect you to be less human in order to be more spiritual.**

That matters in seasons of burnout. Some women have learned to override every signal of depletion until the body or soul finally forces a stop. Then shame rushes in, as though needing care were a failure of character. But the Lord who made us is not scandalized by our limits. He does not step back when we admit them. Very often He draws nearer there.

To live faithfully is not to pretend you need nothing. It is to become honest about what restoration requires and to receive it without contempt. Sleep, nourishment, quiet, tears, friendship, help, medical care, and gentler rhythms all belong in the same life with prayer. **Sometimes grace looks less like sudden strength and more like being cared for by God in very ordinary ways.**

sections.heading: Prayer
sections.body: Merciful Lord, You know that I am dust, and You know how quickly I blame myself when I slow down. Thank You that You are gentler with me than I am with myself. When You see me worn down, You do not rush first to condemn. You show compassion. Heal the part of me that is afraid of limits, and give me humility to receive care and help. If I keep driving myself too hard, stop me in love. Shepherd me into honest rest and teach me to live as a human being, not as a machine. In Jesus’ name, amen.

verses.text: And as he lay and slept under a juniper tree, behold, then an angel touched him, and said unto him, Arise and eat… And he arose, and did eat and drink, and went in the strength of that meat forty days and forty nights.
Like as a father pitieth his children, so the LORD pitieth them that fear him. For he knoweth our frame; he remembereth that we are dust.

days.title: Recovery Begins by Reconnecting to God, Not to Demands

verse.text: Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest… For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.

sections.heading: Guided Reflection
paragraphs: Many people think of recovery as a way to become efficient again. They rest so they can return stronger to the same exhausting cycle. In that kind of life, even rest becomes an instrument of performance. It is valuable only if it helps you produce more.

Jesus offers something better. He tells the weary to come to Him. He speaks of a lighter yoke, not merely a quicker recovery. John 15 deepens that invitation by using the language of abiding. This means renewal is not mainly a scheduling problem. It is a relational one. A woman can step away from noise for a little while and still return to the same inner striving if she never truly returns to God.

**Real restoration does not begin when you are productive again. It begins when your heart is reattached to the One who decides what is truly yours to carry.** That changes the spirit of an entire day. It changes what feels urgent, what gets released, and what no longer deserves to live in your shoulders and nerves as constant pressure.

The deeper goal is not to become your old high-capacity self again. It is to become a woman who lives more closely connected to Christ than to demand. Such a life still works, still serves, still loves. But it no longer draws all its strength from pressure. It begins to draw strength from communion, discernment, and peace.

sections.heading: Prayer
sections.body: Lord Jesus, I thank You that You are not a master who pushes me harder, but the Savior who invites me to come and rest. I often want quick recovery only so I can return to the same strain. Deliver me from that cycle. Show me which burdens come from You and which ones I have refused to release. Let me return to You before I return to demand. Without You, even my effort leaves me empty. Keep me abiding in You, and let strength rise from communion rather than strain. In Jesus’ name, amen.

verses.text: Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest… For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.
Abide in me, and I in you… for without me ye can do nothing.
